coredata.dc:title: pH-controlled construction of 2D and 3D micro-nano hybrid carbon architectures with fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission for white-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Jian
Zhang, Xin
Gu, Rong
Gu, Zimeng
Lu, Xiulian
Zhang, Zhengyu
Yang, Hongyu
Jing, Su
coredata.dc:description: Developing nanocarbon-based white-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with high energy conversion efficiency is still extremely challenging due to the issue of Kasha's rule and serious self-aggregation caused luminescent quenching of nanocarbon in a solid-state. Herein, the synergistic strategy of framework and intercalator between nanosheets and carbon dots (CDs) was adopted. Three novel 2D nanosheets, 3D nanoflowers and 3D parasa consocia-like micro-nano carbon architectures decorated with dispersive CDs were successfully fabricated through one-step hydrothermal reaction at different pH medium. The pH-regulated mechanisms of morphology and structure, via degree modulation of condensation and carbonization, were further proposed. All samples exhibited bright white emission, derived from fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission. The hybrid emission made sufficient utilization of single and triplet excitons with the quantum yield of 16%–30%. The superior white light with CIE coordinates of (0.331,0.334) was recorded for neutral sample (p-CNDs-N), ascribed to the multiple energy level transitions from polydisperse luminescence centers. The luminous efficiency was remarkably improved in HCl (p-CNDs-H) and NaOH treated samples (p-CNDs-OH), resulting from inner carbonization, external cross-linked network and the passivation of surface defect sites. This is the first example for pH-controlled fabrication of multi-dimension hybrid nanocarbon structures and pH-controlled fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission for highly efficient carbon-based WLEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
3D carbon architectures
Hybrid fluorescence/phosphorescence
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of p-CNDs-H nanosheets and p-CNDs-OH parasa consocia-like micro-nano carbon architectures
Synthesis and characterization of 2D and 3D nanocarbon architectures
Photophysical properties of p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for synthesizing 2D nanosheets (p-CNDs-H), 3D nanoflowers (p-CNDs-N) and 3D parasa consocia-like micro-nano carbon materials (p-CNDs-OH). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images of (a) p-CNDs-H, (c) p-CNDs-N and (e) p-CNDs-OH. HR-TEM images of (b) p-CNDs-H, (d) p-CNDs-N and (f) p-CNDs-OH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of (a) p-CNDs-H, (b) p-CNDs-N and (c) p-CNDs-OH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra of p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N, p-CNDs-OH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH. Fluorescence (FL) and afterglow spectra of (b) p-CNDs-H (c) p-CNDs-N and (d) p-CNDs-OH (λex = 365 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Photographs of p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH before and after switching off the 365 nm UV excitation from 0 to 7 s at room temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Corresponding fluorescence and phosphorescence decay profiles of (a) p-CNDs-H, (b) p-CNDs-N and (c) p-CNDs-OH (d) The proposed mechanism of fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission for the three samples. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

EL spectra of the WLEDs coating (a) p-CNDs-H, (b) p-CNDs-N and (c) p-CNDs-OH powers excited by a 365 nm chip, respectively. Insert: the corresponding illumination photographs. (d) CIE chromaticity coordinates of WLEDs coating different phosphors. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: pH-controlled construction of 2D and 3D micro-nano hybrid carbon architectures with fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission for white-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, 224051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing nanocarbon-based white-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with high energy conversion efficiency is still extremely challenging due to the issue of Kasha's rule and serious self-aggregation caused luminescent quenching of nanocarbon in a solid-state. Herein, the synergistic strategy of framework and intercalator between nanosheets and carbon dots (CDs) was adopted. Three novel 2D nanosheets, 3D nanoflowers and 3D parasa consocia-like micro-nano carbon architectures decorated with dispersive CDs were successfully fabricated through one-step hydrothermal reaction at different pH medium. The pH-regulated mechanisms of morphology and structure, via degree modulation of condensation and carbonization, were further proposed. All samples exhibited bright white emission, derived from fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission. The hybrid emission made sufficient utilization of single and triplet excitons with the quantum yield of 16%–30%. The superior white light with CIE coordinates of (0.331,0.334) was recorded for neutral sample (p-CNDs-N), ascribed to the multiple energy level transitions from polydisperse luminescence centers. The luminous efficiency was remarkably improved in HCl (p-CNDs-H) and NaOH treated samples (p-CNDs-OH), resulting from inner carbonization, external cross-linked network and the passivation of surface defect sites. This is the first example for pH-controlled fabrication of multi-dimension hybrid nanocarbon structures and pH-controlled fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission for highly efficient carbon-based WLEDs.

ce:section.ce:para: White-emitting diodes (WLEDs) based on single-component materials have become a powerful competitor for the next-generation of lighting source [1–3]. Since they can effectively avoid suffering from disadvantages present in traditional multi-component WLEDs, such as color/phase separation, self-absorption and fabricated complexity of mixed emitters. Recently, sufficient utilization of singlet and triplet states generated various excitons from the materials has been considered as the effective strategy for the construction of single-component WLEDs with high energy conversion efficiency. For instance, Yang and Tian's groups made both singlet and triplet states from N, P or B doped CDs to produce fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission, covering the entire visible spectra from 400 to 700 nm for WLEDs with a quantum yield in the range of 21%–46% [4–7]. However, because of the issue of serious self-aggregation caused luminescent quenching of nanocarbon in a solid-state, WLEDs showing fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission reported so far are rather limited and mainly restrained to rare-earth materials [8], organometallic complexes [9] and organic compounds [1], which suffering from toxicity, high cost, low photostability and complicated fabrication process. Thus, it is highly desirable for single-component WLEDs featured with stable, low cost, nontoxic and high energy conversion efficiency.

Carbon dots (CDs), 0D carbon nanomaterials, have been proved to be a promising type of luminescent nanomaterials owing to their low toxicity, low cost, light-bleaching resistance and facile preparation process [10]. However, most the CDs encounter the obstacle of the intrinsic self-aggregation caused luminescent quenching (ACQ) phenomenon, mainly stemming from the resonance energy transfer and π-π interactions, especially in a high concentration and solid-state [11]. This is a key challenge for successful application in solid-state emitting devices. To overcome the ACQ phenomenon, there several typical methods have been made, including surface passivation/functionalization [12] and embedding/dispersing CDs in various matrices, such as polymer [13], silica [14], zeolite [14], boron oxynitride [5] and porous materials [15]. 3D architectures can utmostly expose the surface-active sites, effectively avoid the aggregation of individual nanosheets, and strongly guarantee structural durability, highly desired in high-performance optical devices, supercapacitors and electric energy storage [16–18]. They were mainly including doped graphene sheets with 3D graphene-based macrostructures (GBMs) [19], 3D GBMs co-constructed with other carbonaceous materials [20], and incorporation of metal (oxides/sulfides) nanoparticles into 3D GBMs [21]. Most of them were synthesized with hydrothermal-reduction, chemical-reduction, cross-linking-agent or direct assembly methods and template-directed assembly methods [22,23]. However, the development of a facile and effective method to fabricate 2D and 3D carbon frameworks with CDs decoration in situ for tunable luminescence is nearly unexplored.

In this work, we report a facile route to synthesize three novel 2D nanosheets, 3D nanoflowers and 3D parasa consocia-like micro-nano carbon architectures via a one-step hydrothermal reaction of melamine and citric acid at different pH medium. Importantly, all of them were embedded by uniformly dispersed CDs. The mechanism of pH-dependent structures was also investigated. Moreover, all samples exhibited bright white light emission derived from fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission with an overall PLQY of 16%–30%, similar to the previously reported W-CNQDs [4], and were successfully applied to WLEDs.

Melamine (MA, ≥99%) and citric acid monohydrate (CA, ≥99.5%) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36%–38%) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was widely used throughout the experiments.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH was investigated by a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA). The binding energy scale was calibrated by the standard value of C1s at 284.6 eV. The morphology and particle size of synthesized p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH were recorded on the JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Ltd., Japan). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected using a Bruker D8 discover X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The FT-IR spectra were obtained on a Bruker Vector 22 FT-IR spectrophotometer using KBr pellets. UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on the UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence, afterglow emission and excitation spectra were recorded on a HITACHI F-7000 at ambient conditions. Fluorescence and afterglow lifetimes were evaluated using a FluoroLog 3-TSCPC (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., Japan). The absolute quantum yield was determined using a quantum yield accessory, including an integrating sphere (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc. Japan). Photographs of FL and afterglow emission recorded with an iPhone 8 under excitation of the 365 nm hand-held UV lamp (3 W, Taoyuan, China). The emission spectrum, CIE coordinates, CCT, color purity, and luminous efficiency of the WLEDs were performed by integrating sphere (PMS-50, Photo-E-INFO CO., LTD, Hangzhou, China).

0.1134 g MA and 0.0523 g CA were dissolved in 15 mL deionized water. After the ultrasound treatment for 10 min, the mixed solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 4 h, and naturally cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the raw p-CNDs-N power was directly obtained through filtration by a filter paper (φ10-φ15 μm). After washing twice with DI water and ethanol, respectively, and drying at 60 °C in vacuum, the target product p-CNDs-N was obtained as a fluffy white powder in the yield of 78%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of p-CNDs-H nanosheets and p-CNDs-OH parasa consocia-like micro-nano carbon architectures
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of p-CNDs-H nanosheets and p-CNDs-OH parasa consocia-like carbon architectures, pH regulation of the system was conducted. Typically, the pH of mixtures solution containing 0.1134 g MA and 0.0523 g CA was adjusted to 6.0 and 8.0 with a saturated solution of HCl and NaOH, respectively. Then followed similar synthesized processes as the preparation of p-CNDs-N. The corresponding target products p-CNDs-H and p-CNDs-OH were obtained as a white and bright yellow powder in the yield of 81% and 84%, respectively.

UV chips with central emission of 365 nm were chosen to fabricate WLEDs. p-CNDs-N, p-CNDs-H and p-CNDs-OH were mixed with 0.5 g epoxy resin glue (mass ratios of glue A and glue B was 1:3), respectively. Then the mixture was coated onto the UV chips, heated at 120 °C for 2 h and dried in the atmosphere, sequentially. Finally, these photoluminescent materials were firmly fixed on the chips and the fabrication of WLEDs was accomplished. All operating voltage of WLEDs was 3.0 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of 2D and 3D nanocarbon architectures
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the 2D carbon nanosheets (p-CNDs-H), 3D carbon nanoflowers (p-CNDs-N) and 3D carbon parasa consocia architectures (p-CNDs-OH) were prepared by a pH-controlled hydrothermal treatment of melamine (MA) and citric acid monohydrate (CA), and their morphology was detected by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The typical procedures are illustrated in Fig. 1 . When synthesis at 200 °C, with all other conditions kept constant, produced nanoflower-like p-CNDs-N. As shown in Fig. 2 c, such nanoflowers were built from primary loosely-packed nanosheets through self-assembly, and were highly uniform with an average diameter of about 148 nm. The HR-TEM micrograph showed that the thickness of the primary nanosheet of nanoflowers was ∼4.5 nm (Fig. S1b). Upon the mixture solution in a weak acidic environment (pH 6.0) at 200 °C, nanosheet-like p-CNDs-H was produced with a thickness of ∼13.2 nm (Fig. 2a and S1a). Interestingly, with the pH changes of the mixture solution to weak alkaline environment (pH 8.0), the parasa consocia-like architecture of p-CNDs-OH was well constructed (Fig. 2e). It is obvious that the packed nanosheets of parasa consocia-like p-CNDs-OH showed more compact and thicker (∼9.5 nm) (Fig. S1c) than that of p-CNDs-N under the higher pH condition, which might be helpful for self-assembly of nanosheets to fabricate the 3D framework. Furthermore, it also could be clearly seen from the HR-TEM micrographs of p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH that large amounts of dark dots were highly dispersed on each nanosheet, and they could not be washed away, suggesting that these resulting carbon materials comprised nanosheets, nanoflowers and micro-nano parasa consocia architectures with embedded carbon nanodots (Fig. 2b, d and 2f). Apparently, the nanosheets severed as a framework to promote uniform in situ decoration of CDs, simultaneously, the CDs also acted as an intercalator to separate the nanosheets to effectively prevent the aggregation and restacking of nanosheets in such hybrid structures. It suggests that the synergistic effect between CDs and nanosheets is crucial in this stable 2D or 3D framework. Given that only the initial pH differed among the three samples, the pH of the reaction greatly influenced the morphology and structures of the three hybrid carbon materials.

It should be noted that these unique 2D and 3D structures of CDs/nanosheets hybrid materials with the appropriate aggregation degree may be prone to facilitate the dispersion of embedded CDs to resist fluorescent self-quenching in a solid-state, but also promote to create afterglow emission by restraining nonradiative transitions of T1 via confining the motion of the groups on CDs with their nanosheets matrices. So, their optical properties were subsequently systematically investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–Vis spectra of p-CNDs-N in the solid-state displayed a strong absorption peak at 230 nm and a broad absorption band centered at 356 nm assigning to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains in the carbonic core center of embedded CDs and n-π* transitions of the carbon-heteroatom double bond on their surfaces (C=O/C=N), respectively [29]. Notably, in the range of 400–800 nm, the optical density and the absorption range was obviously enhanced and expanded, after H+ or OH− treatment, owing to the electron rearrangement induced by plenty and relative aggregated carboxyl on CDs surfaces [30] (Fig. 5 ). The as-prepared p-CNDs-N exhibited bright white light with an overall absolute PLQY of 16% under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, and intense yellow-green afterglow emission with a QY of 6% after the UV lamp cease, respectively. The afterglow was easily recognized by the naked eye under ambient conditions within 4 s (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence emission spectrum of p-CNDs-N exhibited blue, green and red three emission bands peaking at 423, 512 and 601 nm, respectively, and the delayed spectrum of that exhibited yellow-green broad emission band at 533 nm. To determine the emission centers of the hybrid structures, the fluorescence and afterglow spectra of CA and MA molecules were measured. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence and afterglow intensities of CA and MA molecules could be ignored in comparison with that of the hybrid architectures. Thus, the possibility that the emission come from CA or MA molecules could be excluded. It implied that multiple energy level transitions from polydisperse luminescence centers of embedded CDs were produced by the complex condensation and carbonization process [31,32]. Up to now, the most accepted luminous mechanisms of CDs come down to surface-state-controlled, carbon core state-controlled, molecule state-controlled and their synergistic effect [10]. To distinguish which one is the most likely mechanism, we analyzed the fluorescence excitation spectra and afterglow excitation spectra of the three kinds of micro-nano hybrid carbon architectures (Fig. S5), and compared the excitation peak positions with their absorbance band positions (Fig. 5a). As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence excitation spectra and afterglow excitation spectra of the three samples centered at around 360 nm, overlapping with their absorbance bands, which are ascribed to the above described n-π* transitions of C=O/C=N on the surfaces of CDs (Fig. 5a). It suggests that the surface states (C=O/C=N) of embedded CDs were principally responsible for the photoluminescence origin of the micro-nano hybrid carbon architectures [10,33]. It can also be deduced that these four emission bands were composed of short-lived and long-lived species in their white-light emission process, and were responsible for the broad emission band, covering the entire visible region. The average lifetimes of the photoluminescence signals at 423 and 533 nm were calculated to be 5.64 ns and 378 ms, respectively, based on the following Eqn (1) (Fig. 7 b):
(1)
τ
a v g
= Σ
α i
τ i 2
/ Σ
α i
τ i
The afterglow lifetime is comparable to that of reported CDs@ zeolite (τavg = 350–860 ms) and CDs@SiO2 composites (τavg = 252 ms) [34–36].

After pH treatment, the obtained p-CNDs-H still gave out bright white light but with a little cold, and p-CNDs-OH gave out impure white light with a little green. However, they had a higher overall absolute PLQY up to 25% and 30%, and stronger yellow-green afterglow emission with a QY of 8% and 11%, lasting for 5 s and 7 s, respectively. The increase trend of PLQY and QY may be associated with their inner carbonization or external crosslinked network structures. Usually, the number of defects in centric carbon core structure would increase with the increasing of the oxygen element, which rising more conjugated π-domains as the carbon core luminescence centers [37,38]. In addition, different from the serious ACQ phenomenon caused by RET and π-π interaction of traditional CDs particles, the special CDs/nanosheets hybrid structures facilitated the dispersion of embedded CDs. In these hybrid structures, the nanosheets severed as a framework for uniform in situ decoration of CDs, while the CDs also acted as an intercalator to separate the nanosheets and restack the nanosheets to effectively prevent the CDs aggregation. Besides, large amount C=O groups out of the particles were prone to further restrain π-π interactions of CDs from suppressing the luminescence self-quenching in the solid-state [39]. These give a reasonable explanation to the improvement of PLQY and QY in p-CNDs-H featured with higher carbonation degrees and more C=O groups. As the p-CNDs-OH, complex covalently crosslinked frameworks and intraparticle hydrogen bonds restricted the vibration and rotation to suppress the non-radiation relaxation de-excitation process, and to prompt the radiative transition probability for improving the enhanced fluorescence or afterglow [40,41]. Both the fluorescence emission spectra of p-CNDs-H and p-CNDs-OH exhibited blue and green dual broad emission bands peaking at 423/512 nm and 420/526 nm, and the delayed spectra exhibited yellow-green broad emission bands centered at 521 nm and 530 nm, respectively (Fig. 5b and d). Apparently, the maximum fluorescence emission peaks of p-CNDs-H and p-CNDs-OH in solid had an obvious blueshift, a larger intensity gap between the maximum and shoulder peak, and a less significant component of red emission, compared with that of p-CNDs-N. This may result from the stronger UV–Vis absorbance after 550 nm and resulting in a colder color temperature of their emitting light. Moreover, the impure white light in p-CNDs-OH possibly suffered from the relatively prominent intensity from its yellow-green afterglow component. However, the excitation-independent emission of fluorescence and afterglow was a common feature in these three samples, within the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 380 nm, suggesting the formation of a self-passivated structure by surface functional groups (Fig. S6) [42]. Importantly, the passivation of surface defect sites just reduced the quenching of the excitons caused by the surface trap, further contributing to the enhanced photoluminescence [41]. Among these emitters, their average fluorescence lifetimes gradually shorten from 5.71 ns (p-CNDs-H), 5.64 ns (p-CNDs-N) to 4.57 ns (p-CNDs-OH) along with the pH increasing (Fig. 7). It could be attributed to the resonance energy transfer (RET) effect of CDs embedded on nanosheets, arising from the gradual aggregation of CDs/sheets and competing with the radiative transition [11,43,44]. However, in terms of afterglow lifetimes, it presented an exactly opposite tendency with the longest lifetime of 386 ms in the p-CNDs-OH emitter and the shortest one 314 ms in the p-CNDs-H emitter (Fig. 7). Because after alkaline treatment, the complex hydrogen bonds between inter CDs/sheets and intra CDs/sheets were produced through deprotonated amino/amide nitrogen and C=N bonds on CDs/sheets, and then stabilized the triplet excitons for afterglow generation [45,46]. Instead of alkaline treatment, the hydrogen bonds were disturbed by protonated above bonds. Ultimately, such low pH thus shortened their afterglow lifetimes [47]. Nevertheless, it is detrimental to the photoluminescence of the carbon-based emitters with the excessive acid or alkaline treatment, which may be associated with the over-carbonization induced consumption of edge states or insufficient carbonization induced deficiency of emitted species (Fig. S7).

Furthermore, the less overlap between delayed emission spectrum and solid fluorescence one (around 430 nm) (Fig. 5), and the CIE chromaticity coordinates of greenish-blue FL and yellow-green afterglow for three samples (Fig. S8) both implied that the afterglow emission was attributed to the phosphorescence process. Thus, the singlet (S1)-Triplet (T1) energy gaps (ΔE ST) were further investigated through the low temperature (77K) fluorescence and delayed spectra of three composites (Fig. S9). As expected, all calculated ΔE ST values ranged from 0.38 to 0.68 eV, which were suitable for effective intersystem crossing (ISC) processes to populate T1 excitions [48]. Since all calculated ΔE ST values exceeded 0.3 eV, suggesting that the thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) process was reasonably excluded (Fig. 7d) [49]. As shown in Fig. S10, the afterglow intensity of three samples relatively declined as the temperature increasing from 173 to 273 K, which is characteristic for the phosphorescence emissions. In addition, the afterglow lifetimes were sensitive to temperature. All of them revealed a decreasing trend upon increasing the temperature from 148 K to 298 K, further confirming that the afterglow emission was attributed to the phosphorescence process (Fig. S11) [50]. These luminescent results revealed that the multiple luminescence centers of embedded CDs could exert two independent electronic transitions (S1 and S′1) with a large energy gap between the two singlet energy levels. Such different energy excited states produced a single-luminophore dual fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, a relatively small energy gap between S1 and T1 allowed a direct ISC process to generate a low-energy phosphorescence emission (Fig. 7d). These pathways were favorable to the breakthrough of the Kasha's rule which states that the radiative transitions can only occur at the lowest excited level [51].

Notedly, the fact that the smaller ΔE ST of p-CNDs-H (0.38 eV) and p-CNDs-OH(0.61 eV) than that of p-CNDs-N (0.68 eV) should be related to the abundant C=O groups around the CDs embedded on the nanosheets, more favorable for effective spin-orbit coupling ISC process [4]. As a result, a better afterglow performance from pH-treated samples (p-CNDs-H and p-CNDs-OH) was observed, consistent with their nature of QY.

The efficient fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission of samples, covered the whole visible range, coupled with facile preparation, low cost and environmental-friendliness were compelling for their potential application in high-performance WLEDs. So, p-CNDs-H, p-CNDs-N and p-CNDs-OH were role as single phosphors and directly coated with AB glue on the surface of 365 nm UV-chips, respectively. The bright white illumination images and emission spectrum of as-constructed WLEDs at 3 V driving current were shown in Fig. 8 . Notedly, yellow-green phosphorescence still could be captured by the naked eye after turning off the power within 5 s (Fig. S12). With regard to the electroluminescence (EL) spectrum of p-CNDs-N, three fluorescence/phosphorescence hybrid broad peaks centered at 449, 521 and 611 nm could be clearly observed. Apparently, the EL spectra displayed a significant red-shift compared with its steady-state PL, attributed to the inevitable consecutive reabsorption and reemission for the high contents of the sample in the color conversion layer [4,52]. Moreover, a little enhanced blue emission part was also observed in its EL spectrum, which may be associated with the thermal radiation induced the intensive process around 450 nm (Fig. S10). Thanks to the intensity distributed equilibrium of three primary colors emitting from fluorescence/phosphorescence hybrid, a white colorimetric purity with a Commission International d’Eclairage Chromaticity diagrams (CIE) coordinate at (0.331, 0.334) was achieved, which was quite close to ultra-pure white coordinate (0.330, 0.330). Furthermore, the WLEDs based on p-CNDs-N also featured with a high color rendering index (CRI) of 87.5, which was comparable to that of many reported QD and lead halide perovskites-based WLEDs, and meeting the requirements of indoor lighting (CRI>80) [53]. Besides, its correlated color temperature (CCT) was determined as 5030 K, approaching the so-called “warm” white light source (<4500 K) [54]. Unlike that of p-CNDs-N-based WLEDs, both the EL spectra of WLEDs prepared from p-CNDs-H and p-CNDs-OH exhibited two distinct broad peaks, centered at 444 nm/545 nm and 422 nm/539 nm, ascribed to the fluorescence/phosphorescence hybrid emission, respectively. Because of this change in the intensity distributions, the red component was relatively weakened and a more greenish appeared. Therefore, compared with that of p-CNDs-N phosphor, p-CNDs-H- and p-CNDs–OH–based WLEDs afforded a colder white/yellowish-green color with CIE coordinates of (0.306, 0.342) and (0.306, 0.351), CCT of 6240 K and 6409 K, and lower CRI of 80 and 64, respectively. However, these two WLEDs constructed by pH-treated samples greatly improved their luminous efficiency (LE) of 107%–227% (LE = 17.4 lm W−1 for p-CNDs-H and LE = 27.5 lm W−1 for p-CNDs-OH) compared with the p-CNDs-N-based WLEDs (LE = 8.4 lm W−1), which are comparable to previously reported values (Table S2). Furthermore, the as-synthesized WLEDs showed good chromatic stability and high device stability after a continuous 48 h operation (Figs. S13 and S14).

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated a pH-controlled hydrothermal method for the high yield synthesis of 2D nanosheets, 3D nanoflower and 3D parasa consocia-like nanocarbon architectures with embedded CDs via the degree modulation of condensation and carbonization. Such three kinds of CDs (intercalator)/nanosheets (framework) hybrid 2D and 3D structures not only stabilized the multi-dimensional architectures, but also facilitated the dispersion of embedded CDs to resist ACQ phenomenon in a solid-state and raised fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-mode emission for white light emissions with an overall PLQY of 16%–30% and afterglow lifetimes of 314–386 ms. WLEDs were fabricated by integrating phosphors in UV-LED chips. The superior white light purity with CIE coordinates and a CRI of (0.331, 0.334) and 87.5 was obtained in p-CNDs-N phosphor-based WLED. Moreover, the luminous efficiency was remarkably improved in p-CNDs-H and p-CNDs-OH phosphor-based WLEDs, but sacrificed the color purity slightly and rendering index. This study might a new avenue for the direct construction of novel nanocarbon architectures and tunable carbon-based WLEDs with high-efficiency.

Jian Qu: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Xin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources. Rong Gu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Resources. Zimeng Gu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Supervision. Xiulian Lu: Writing – review & editing. Zhengyu Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Hongyu Yang: Writing – review & editing. Su Jing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by funding for school-level research projects of Yancheng Institute of Technology (xjr2019021 and xjr2019020), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20191041), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22175090) and the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2021712).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2023.118355.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Space conjugation induced white light and room-temperature phosphorescence from simple organic small molecules: single-component WLED driven by both UV and blue chips

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zheng, Y. Huang, D. Xiao, S. Yang, Z. Lin, Q. Ling, Space conjugation induced white light and room-temperature phosphorescence from simple organic small molecules: single-component WLED driven by both UV and blue chips, Mater. Chem. Front. 5 (2021) 6960-6968.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ce3+-Doped garnet phosphors: composition modification, luminescence properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xia, A. Meijerink, Ce3+-Doped garnet phosphors: composition modification, luminescence properties and applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 46 (2017) 275-299.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-phase white-light-emitting and photoluminescent color-tuning coordination assemblies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Pan, W.M. Liao, S.Y. Yin, S.S. Sun, C.Y. Su, Single-phase white-light-emitting and photoluminescent color-tuning coordination assemblies, Chem. Rev. 118 (2018) 8889-8935.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence-phosphorescence dual emissive carbon nitride quantum dots show 25% white emission efficiency enabling single-component WLEDs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Yuan, F. Yuan, X. Li, Y. Li, L. Fan, S. Yang, Fluorescence-phosphorescence dual emissive carbon nitride quantum dots show 25% white emission efficiency enabling single-component WLEDs, Chem. Sci. 10 (2019) 9801-9806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Achieving 46% efficient white-light emissive carbon dot-based materials by enhancing phosphorescence for single-component white-light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Li, Y. Li, S. Meng, J. Yang, Y. Qin, J. Tan, S. Qu, Achieving 46% efficient white-light emissive carbon dot-based materials by enhancing phosphorescence for single-component white-light-emitting diodes, J. Mater. Chem. C 9 (2021) 6796-6801.

Meng, Gram-scale synthesis of 41% efficient single-component white-light-emissive carbonized polymer dots with hybrid fluorescence/phosphorescence for white light-emitting diodes, Adv. Sci. 7 (2020) 1902688.
